text.title: User Interview Process and Question Design
text.summary: Interview types, recruitment criteria, question design principles, and synthesis techniques.

section.title: Interview Types
section.p: Exploratory (45-60 min) — understand the problem space and user needs. Generative (30-45 min) — generate ideas about possible solutions. Evaluative (30-45 min) — test concepts or early-stage designs. Contextual (60-90 min) — observe users in their actual environment.

rule.statement: Write research questions (what you need to learn) before writing interview questions (what you will ask).
rule.rationale: Starting with interview questions produces survey-like guides; starting with research objectives produces guides that follow the user's answers.

section.title: Recruitment Criteria
section.p: 5-8 participants per distinct user segment is the practical threshold for theme saturation. Recruit from: customer database, user panels, social media, professional recruiters. Mix demographics; include both current users and prospects.

section.title: Question Design

rule.statement: Use open-ended questions that ask about past behavior, not hypothetical future behavior.
rule.rationale: Hypothetical questions ("would you use...") produce aspirational answers, not behavioral evidence. Past behavior predicts actual usage.

example.note: "Tell me about the last time you [did X]." — anchors to a specific real experience with retrievable detail.
"Would you use a feature that does Y?" — hypothetical; users say yes to be helpful, not because they would actually use it.
"Walk me through your typical process for [task]." — generates behavioral context the designer can act on.
"Don't you think the app is too complicated?" — leading; suggests the answer and biases the response.

section.p: Probing follow-ups: "Why do you think that happened?", "Can you give me a specific example?", "Tell me more about that."

section.title: Synthesis Techniques
section.p: Affinity mapping: write each observation on a separate note, group similar notes, name each group, identify patterns across groups.
Insight statement format: "[User type] needs [need] because [motivation/reason]. Currently, [pain point/gap]."

rule.statement: Separate "what users said" from "what we infer" in every insight statement.
rule.rationale: Collapsing observation and inference produces insights that sound like research findings but are actually interpretations; stakeholders cannot challenge what they cannot see the evidence for.
